cards.text: The term {{c1::code coverage}} is used to describe how much of the source code of a program is executed while testing.
{{c1::Statement coverage}} refers to the percentage of statements in your code that have been exercised by your testing routines.

cards.back: Branch coverage refers to the percentage of branches that have been executed, with each possible branch (true/false) counted separately. An if statement has 2 branches, a switch can have many branches.

cards.question: How many function calls are needed for 100% branch coverage of this function?
cards.explanation: You need two calls: one where x > y (e.g., foo(1, 0)) and one where x <= y (e.g., foo(0, 1)). This tests both the true and false branches of the if statement.

cards.question: How many function calls are needed for 100% branch coverage?
cards.explanation: You need three calls: compare(1, 0) for x > y, compare(0, 1) for x < y, and compare(0, 0) for x == y. This covers all three branches.

cards.text: Best practice: Aim for {{c1::100% branch coverage}} of your code.

{{c1::Loop coverage}} (informally called the {{c2::0, 1, 2 test}}) says that if you have a loop, you should ensure it works properly when it iterates {{c3::0 times}}, {{c4::1 time}}, and {{c5::2 times}}.

cards.front: Why is testing a loop with 2 iterations sufficient for all larger iteration counts?
cards.back: If the loop works correctly for 2 iterations, it should work correctly for all iterations greater than 2, since the loop mechanism is proven to work multiple times. A loop can't execute a negative number of times.

cards.text: Best practice: Use the {{c1::0, 1, 2 test}} to ensure your loops work correctly with different number of iterations.

cards.concept: Categories of input values to test for integers
cards.explanation:
When testing functions that accept integer parameters, make sure you've considered how your function handles:
  • Negative values
  • Zero
  • Positive values
  • Overflow (if relevant)
Testing representative values from each category helps ensure your function handles all cases correctly.

cards.front: What are good double values to test with to catch precision issues?
cards.back: Test with 0.1 and -0.1 (numbers slightly larger than expected) and 0.7 and -0.7 (numbers slightly smaller than expected) to catch rounding/precision problems.

cards.concept: Categories of string input to test
cards.explanation:
When testing functions that accept string parameters, make sure you've considered:
  • Empty string ("")
  • Alphanumeric string ("abc123")
  • Strings with whitespace - leading, trailing, and inner
  • Strings that are all whitespace (" ")
Each category can expose different bugs in string handling code.
